lessons.title: Why save money?
lessons.content:
Saving money means keeping some of your money instead of spending it all. When you save, you can buy bigger things later, or have money ready if something unexpected happens.

Imagine you get ₹100 pocket money every week. If you spend all of it on snacks and games, you will have nothing left. But if you save even ₹30 each week, after 10 weeks you will have ₹300 — enough for something really special!
keyPoints: Saving means setting aside money for later
Even saving a small amount adds up over time
Savings give you a safety net for surprises

lessons.title: The piggy bank rule
lessons.content:
A great rule for saving is called Save First, Spend Later. Every time you get money, put some aside before you spend anything. This is called paying yourself first.

A popular way to split your money is the 50/30/20 rule — spend 50% on things you need (food, school), 30% on things you want (games, treats), and save 20%. If you get ₹100, that means saving ₹20 every time!
keyPoints: Save before you spend — not after
The 50/30/20 rule helps split your money wisely
Even ₹5 saved today becomes more tomorrow

lessons.title: Setting a savings goal
lessons.content:
Goals make saving fun! Instead of saving with no plan, pick something you really want — maybe a new game, a book, or a gift for someone you love.

Write down your goal, how much it costs, and how long it will take to save. When you can see a clear plan, saving feels exciting instead of boring. Checking off each week you save feels great!
keyPoints: Pick a specific thing you are saving for
Know exactly how much it costs
Track your progress every week to stay motivated

questions.question: What does saving money mean?
options: Spending all your money quickly
Keeping some money aside for later
Giving all your money to friends
Borrowing money from the bank
questions.explanation: Saving means setting aside money now instead of spending it all, so you have it available for something important later.

questions.question: In the 50/30/20 rule, what percentage goes to savings?
questions.explanation: 20% goes to savings. The 50% covers needs like food and school, and 30% covers things you want like games and treats.

questions.question: What does 'paying yourself first' mean?
options: Buying yourself a gift before buying for others
Saving money before spending on anything else
Earning money by doing your own business
Getting paid extra for doing chores
questions.explanation: Paying yourself first means putting money into savings before you spend on anything else — savings come first, spending comes after.

questions.question: You get ₹100 pocket money each week and save ₹25. How much will you save in 4 weeks?
questions.explanation: ₹25 × 4 weeks = ₹100 saved! Small, consistent savings really add up over time.

modules.title: Compound interest
modules.subtitle: Making your money grow on its own

lessons.title: What is interest?
lessons.content:
When you put money in a bank, the bank pays you extra money just for keeping it there. This extra money is called interest.

For example, if you put ₹1,000 in a bank that pays 10% interest per year, after one year you will have ₹1,100. The bank gave you ₹100 just for saving with them — without you doing any extra work!
keyPoints: Interest is money the bank pays you for saving with them
It is shown as a percentage per year (e.g. 10%)
Higher interest rate means you earn more money

lessons.title: The magic of compounding
lessons.content:
Compound interest is when you earn interest on your interest. It sounds simple, but the effect is powerful!

Year 1: ₹1,000 + 10% = ₹1,100
Year 2: ₹1,100 + 10% = ₹1,210
Year 3: ₹1,210 + 10% = ₹1,331

Notice how the amount you earn each year keeps getting bigger? That is compounding. You are earning money on money you already earned. Albert Einstein reportedly called compound interest the eighth wonder of the world.
keyPoints: You earn interest on your previous interest too
The longer you save, the faster your money grows
Starting early gives compounding more time to work

lessons.title: Start early, grow big
lessons.content:
Time is the most important ingredient in compound interest. The earlier you start saving, the more your money grows — even if you save small amounts.

If you save ₹500 every month starting at age 15 at 12% interest, by age 60 you could have over ₹1.5 crore! If you wait until age 30 to start saving the same amount, you would have much less — even though you saved for many years.

The secret? Start today, even if it is a very small amount.
keyPoints: Starting early beats starting with big amounts
Small amounts saved young can grow enormously
Every year you wait costs you more than you think

questions.question: What is interest?
options: A fee you pay to the bank every month
Extra money the bank pays you for saving
The total amount of money in your account
A type of bank account for adults
questions.explanation: Interest is extra money the bank pays you as a reward for keeping your money with them. You earn it without doing any extra work.

questions.question: You have ₹1,000. The bank pays 10% interest per year. How much will you have after 1 year?
What makes compound interest special?
options: It disappears if you withdraw your money
You earn interest on your previously earned interest
It only works for adults with a lot of money
Banks give you compound interest as a gift
questions.explanation: The magic of compounding is that you earn interest on top of your previously earned interest, so your money grows faster and faster over time.

questions.question: Why is starting to save early so important?
options: Banks only accept young savers
You get a discount if you save young
More time means more compounding and bigger growth
Interest rates are higher for young people
questions.explanation: The longer your money compounds, the more it grows. Starting at 15 instead of 30 gives your money 15 extra years to multiply — making a huge difference.

modules.title: How stocks work
modules.subtitle: Owning a piece of a company

lessons.title: What is a stock?
lessons.content:
A stock is a small piece of a company. When a company needs money to grow, it sells tiny pieces of itself to the public. Each piece is called a share or stock.

If you buy one share of a company worth ₹10,000 that has 1,000 shares, you own 1/1000th of that company — you are part owner! If the company does well and grows to ₹20,000, your share is now worth ₹20 instead of ₹10. You made money just by owning it.
keyPoints: A stock is a small ownership piece of a company
When the company grows, your stock value grows too
Stocks are bought and sold on a stock exchange

lessons.title: Why do stock prices change?
lessons.content:
Stock prices go up and down every day based on how people feel about a company's future. If many people think a company will do well, they want to buy its stock — and when more people want to buy, the price goes up.

If bad news comes out about a company, people may sell their stock — when more people sell, the price goes down. This is supply and demand in action!

Famous companies like Reliance, Infosys, and TCS have millions of shares traded every single day.
keyPoints: Prices rise when more people want to buy
Prices fall when more people want to sell
Company news and results affect prices a lot

lessons.title: The stock market and patience
lessons.content:
The stock market can be scary because prices go up and down a lot in the short term. But over long periods — 10, 20, 30 years — the stock market has always gone up.

This is why investors say 'time in the market beats timing the market'. Instead of trying to buy at the perfect moment, the smarter move is to invest regularly and hold for a long time. Patience is the most important skill in investing.
keyPoints: Short-term prices jump up and down — that is normal
Long-term, markets have always trended upward
Investing regularly over time beats trying to guess the market

questions.question: What is a stock?
options: A loan you give to a company
A small ownership piece of a company
Money stored in a bank account
A government savings scheme
questions.explanation: A stock is a small piece of ownership in a company. When you buy a stock, you become a part-owner of that company.

questions.question: A company has 1,000 shares worth ₹10 each. You buy 10 shares. What percentage of the company do you own?
questions.explanation: You own 10 out of 1,000 shares, which is 10/1000 = 1% of the company.

questions.question: A popular company announces record profits. What will likely happen to its stock price?
options: The price will fall because profits are too high
The price will stay exactly the same
The price will rise as more people want to buy
The company will close down
questions.explanation: Good news makes investors confident, so more people want to buy the stock. Higher demand pushes the price up.

questions.question: What does 'time in the market beats timing the market' mean?
options: You should trade stocks every hour
Investing steadily for a long time is better than guessing the perfect moment
The stock market only works in the morning
You need to watch prices all day to make money
questions.explanation: Trying to pick the perfect time to buy and sell is very hard and usually fails. Investing regularly and staying patient over many years consistently works better.
